rows.v: Return on Investment — a measure of how much profit an investment generates relative to its cost, usually expressed as a percentage. The fundamental yardstick of whether something was worth it.
Marketing pushed for the new campaign, but the CFO wanted to see the projected ROI before approving the budget.
A 20th-century accounting term that escaped the finance department to become business shorthand for 'was this worth the money?' applied to almost anything.
Key Performance Indicator — a specific, measurable value that shows how well a team or company is hitting its goals. The numbers leadership actually watches.
Her dashboard tracked three KPIs: monthly active users, churn rate, and average revenue per customer.
Rose to prominence with the data-driven management movement; the word 'key' signals these are the vital few metrics, not every number you could measure.
Two business models: 'B2B' (business-to-business) sells to other companies; 'B2C' (business-to-consumer) sells directly to individuals. They demand very different sales and marketing approaches.
The software started B2C with individual users, then pivoted to B2B to chase larger enterprise contracts.
Shorthand born in the dot-com era, using '2' as a phonetic stand-in for 'to' — a style that also gave us B2B marketplaces and the broader '-2-' naming convention.
The idea that combined efforts produce greater results than the sum of their parts — '1 + 1 = 3.' Often invoked to justify mergers, sometimes more hope than reality.
The merger was sold on synergy: shared infrastructure and cross-selling that would supposedly make the whole worth more than the two halves.
From Greek 'synergos' (working together). A legitimate concept so overused in corporate decks that it became a punchline for empty buzzwords.
Describes a business or system that can grow revenue rapidly without a matching rise in costs. The holy grail for investors — growth that doesn't get more expensive.
Software is scalable: the thousandth customer costs almost nothing to serve, unlike a restaurant that needs another kitchen.
From 'scale' (size, extent). The term exploded with software, where serving more users barely raises costs — the dream investors pay a premium for.
'Vertical' integration or markets means going deep within one industry or owning your supply chain; 'horizontal' means expanding across industries or to competitors' offerings.
They grew vertically by acquiring their own supplier, then horizontally by adding an unrelated product line.
From geometry's axes: 'vertical' moves up and down a single industry's chain; 'horizontal' moves sideways across markets. A staple of strategy vocabulary.
The unique strength or capability that a company does better than anyone else — the thing it should build around and not outsource.
Advised to stick to their core competency, they sold off the side ventures and refocused on what they did best.
Coined by management theorists Prahalad and Hamel in a landmark 1990 article, it became central to corporate strategy thinking in the decade after.
Anyone with an interest in a company's outcomes — not just shareholders, but employees, customers, suppliers, and communities. Broadens 'who matters' beyond the owners.
The new policy weighed every stakeholder: investors wanted profit, employees wanted stability, and customers wanted lower prices.
A deliberate play on 'shareholder' (one who holds shares), widening it to anyone holding a 'stake' in the outcome — central to debates about corporate purpose.
'Revenue' is all the money coming in from sales; 'profit' is what's left after subtracting all costs. A company can have huge revenue and still lose money.
The startup boasted $10M in revenue but zero profit — every dollar earned was spent on growth.
'Revenue' from Old French 'revenir' (to return, come back); 'profit' from Latin 'profectus' (progress, advance). The gap between them is the whole game.
The percentage of revenue left as profit after costs. 'Gross margin' subtracts the cost of goods; 'net margin' subtracts everything. Higher margins mean a healthier business.
Luxury goods run on fat margins, while groceries survive on razor-thin ones and make it up on volume.
From the literal 'margin' (edge) — the slim edge of money remaining after costs are stripped away. The narrower it is, the less room for error.
The actual movement of money in and out of a business over time. A profitable company can still fail if cash doesn't arrive when bills are due.
On paper they were profitable, but a cash-flow crunch — customers paying late — nearly sank them before payday.
A plain compound of 'cash' and 'flow', emphasizing timing over accounting profit. 'Cash is king' became the mantra precisely because of this distinction.
Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization — a measure of a company's core operating profitability, stripping out financing and accounting effects.
Investors focused on EBITDA to compare the two firms' raw operating performance, ignoring their different debt loads.
An acronym from 1980s leveraged-buyout finance, designed to show a company's earning power before the distortions of how it's financed and taxed.
A financial statement showing what a company owns (assets), owes (liabilities), and the difference (equity) at a single point in time. A snapshot of financial health.
Her first move was to read the balance sheet, checking whether assets comfortably covered the company's debts.
Named because the two sides must 'balance': assets always equal liabilities plus equity. One of the three core financial statements.
How easily an asset can be converted to cash without losing value. Cash is perfectly liquid; a building is not. Also a company's ability to cover short-term obligations.
The fund held too many illiquid properties, so when investors wanted out, it couldn't raise cash fast enough.
From 'liquid' (flowing freely) — liquid assets 'flow' easily into cash, while illiquid ones are stuck. The metaphor runs through all of finance.
An accounting method that records revenue and expenses when they're earned or incurred, not when cash actually changes hands. The standard for serious businesses.
Under accrual accounting, the December sale counted in December's books even though the customer paid in January.
From Latin 'accrescere' (to grow, increase). It captures economic activity as it accrues, giving a truer picture than simple cash-in, cash-out accounting.
Spreading the cost of a long-lived asset (like equipment) across the years it's used, rather than expensing it all at once. Reflects wear and aging on the books.
The $50,000 truck was depreciated over five years, deducting $10,000 annually rather than all at purchase.
From Latin 'depretiare' (to lower in price). It matches an asset's cost to the periods that benefit from it — a foundational accounting principle.
A 'bull market' is rising and optimistic; a 'bear market' is falling and pessimistic. The two animals define market mood and investor psychology.
After two years of a roaring bull market, the sudden 20% drop signaled the bears had taken over.
Popularly explained by how each animal attacks — a bull thrusts its horns up, a bear swipes its paws down — though the terms' true origins are debated.
A portion of a company's profits paid out to shareholders, usually quarterly. Income from owning stock, separate from the stock's price rising.
She built a portfolio of dividend stocks, collecting steady quarterly payments regardless of daily price swings.
From Latin 'dividendum' (thing to be divided) — the profit divided among owners. 'Dividend' also survives in math as the number being divided.
Market capitalization — the total value of a company's shares (share price × number of shares). The standard measure of a company's size.
With a $2 trillion market cap, the tech giant was worth more than the entire economy of many countries.
A compound of 'market' and 'capitalization', it reflects what the market collectively values the whole company at, right now.
Price-to-Earnings ratio — a stock's price divided by its earnings per share. A rough gauge of how expensive a stock is relative to its profits.
The stock's sky-high P/E ratio meant investors were paying a steep premium, betting on huge future growth.
A cornerstone of stock valuation since the early 20th century, it answers 'how many dollars am I paying for each dollar of the company's earnings?'
Spreading investments across many assets so no single failure can sink the whole portfolio. The closest thing finance has to a free lunch.
Rather than bet everything on one stock, she practiced diversification across dozens of companies and sectors.
From 'diverse' (varied). The principle 'don't put all your eggs in one basket' formalized into modern portfolio theory, which won a Nobel Prize.
An investment made to offset the risk of another — like insurance for a position. To 'hedge' is to reduce potential loss, often at the cost of some potential gain.
He hedged his big tech bet by buying options that would pay off if the sector crashed.
From the old sense of a 'hedge' as a protective boundary; to 'hedge your bets' (cover yourself on multiple sides) dates back centuries before modern finance adopted it.
Betting that a stock will fall: you borrow shares, sell them, and aim to buy them back cheaper later. Profitable if the price drops, dangerous if it soars.
The short-seller borrowed and sold the overhyped stock, profiting handsomely when it later collapsed.
From 'short' meaning being in deficit — you sell shares you don't own (are 'short' on) and must buy them back. The opposite of going 'long'.
Exchange-Traded Fund — a basket of investments (often tracking an index like the S&P 500) that trades like a single stock. A simple, low-cost way to own the whole market.
Instead of picking winners, she bought a low-fee index ETF and owned a slice of 500 companies at once.
A product innovation of the 1990s; the name describes exactly what it is — a fund that trades (is exchanged) on the market like a stock.
How long a startup can survive before running out of money, based on its current cash and spending rate. Measured in months.
With $1.2M in the bank and $100K monthly burn, they had twelve months of runway to find profitability or raise again.
An aviation metaphor: like a plane needing enough runway to take off, a startup needs enough cash-distance to reach liftoff before it runs out.
The speed at which a startup spends its cash reserves, usually stated per month. High burn shortens runway and raises the pressure to grow or raise funds.
Their burn rate doubled after the hiring spree, alarming investors who watched the runway shrink.
From 'burning through' cash — the vivid image of money going up in flames. A core metric venture investors track obsessively.
The estimated total worth of a company, especially a private startup. Set during funding rounds, it determines how much of the company investors get for their money.
The Series A valued the company at $40M, so the $10M investment bought a 25% stake.
From 'value'. For startups with little revenue, valuation is as much art and negotiation as math — a bet on future potential.
Ownership in a company, usually as shares. Founders, investors, and employees hold equity; its value rises and falls with the company. The currency of startups.
She took a lower salary in exchange for equity, betting that her slice of the company would one day be worth millions.
From Latin 'aequitas' (fairness, evenness) — your equity is your fair share of ownership. In finance it broadly means ownership value after debts.
Venture Capital — money invested in early-stage, high-risk, high-growth startups in exchange for equity. VCs bet on many companies expecting a few huge wins.
After bootstrapping for a year, they raised VC to fuel aggressive expansion and hire fast.
From 'venture' (a risky undertaking) — capital for ventures too risky for banks. The modern VC industry grew up alongside Silicon Valley.
A fundamental change in a startup's strategy, product, or market when the original idea isn't working. A controlled change of direction, not a failure.
The team pivoted from a struggling photo app to the messaging feature users actually loved, and it took off.
From the literal 'pivot' (turning on a fixed point) — the company keeps one foot planted (its team, learnings) while swinging in a new direction. Popularized by the Lean Startup movement.
A privately held startup valued at over $1 billion. Once mythically rare, hence the name; now more common but still a major milestone.
Reaching a $1B valuation, the startup officially became a unicorn, and the press took notice.
Coined by investor Aileen Lee in 2013 to capture how rare and magical billion-dollar startups then were — like the mythical creature.
The point where a product satisfies a strong market demand — customers want it, use it, and tell others. The make-or-break milestone for any startup.
Before product-market fit, growth was a grind; after it, demand pulled the product forward on its own.
Popularized by investor Marc Andreessen, who argued nothing else matters until you find it — the moment the market clearly wants what you built.
Borrowed from tech, it means a person's available time and mental capacity to take on work. 'I don't have the bandwidth' is a polite 'I'm maxed out.'
She wanted to help with the launch but simply didn't have the bandwidth on top of her current projects.
Lifted from telecommunications (the data a channel can carry) and applied to human capacity — one of the most successful tech-to-office metaphor transfers.
'Low-hanging fruit' means the easiest, quickest wins — the gains you can grab with minimal effort before tackling harder problems.
Let's grab the low-hanging fruit first: fixing the checkout bug will boost sales faster than any redesign.
From the literal image of fruit easiest to pick because it hangs within reach — a vivid metaphor for the simplest available opportunities.
To return to a topic or person later, after setting it aside for now. A staple of meeting-speak for 'we'll deal with this another time.'
We're short on time, so let's circle back on the budget question after we've nailed down the timeline.
From the image of looping back around to something — one of the most mocked yet most-used phrases in corporate meetings.
To 'move the needle' is to make a difference big enough to actually register on a gauge — a meaningful, measurable impact, not a trivial one.
A few extra signups won't move the needle; we need a channel that brings in thousands.
From the image of a dial or meter's needle visibly moving — used to separate changes that genuinely matter from cosmetic ones.
A thorough, detailed examination of a topic, going well beyond a surface overview. 'Let's do a deep dive' signals a focused, in-depth session.
The quarterly numbers looked off, so the team scheduled a deep dive to find the root cause.
From the diving metaphor of going below the surface — plunging into the details rather than skimming the top of a subject.
Two deadline abbreviations: 'EOD' (end of day) and 'COB' (close of business). Both mean 'by the end of today,' COB leaning toward standard business hours.
He needed the report by EOD, so she stayed late to send it before midnight.
Office shorthand for setting same-day deadlines; 'COB' descends from banking and trading hours (when business formally 'closes'), while 'EOD' is the looser modern cousin.
